title.rendered: Japan Golden Visa and Property Ownership for Investors

content.rendered:
Japan Golden Visa and Property Ownership for Investors: What You Need to Know in 2025

Japan has long been a dream destination for international investors — a stable economy, world-class infrastructure, and property prices that remain surprisingly accessible compared to other G7 nations. But unlike Portugal or Spain, Japan hasn't historically offered a straightforward "buy property, get residency" pathway.

That changed in April 2025, when Japan officially launched its new Golden Visa program (正式には「特別高度人材制度」), creating a clearer route for high-net-worth individuals to gain long-term residency. If you're an investor from the US, Australia, the UK, or Canada considering Japanese real estate, here's what you need to know about combining property ownership with residency goals.

What Is Japan's Golden Visa Program?

Japan's Golden Visa — officially part of the expanded Highly Skilled Professional (HSP) visa framework — allows qualifying investors to obtain residency based on their assets, income, and contributions to Japan's economy. Unlike traditional work visas, this pathway doesn't require employment with a Japanese company.

Key Eligibility Requirements

To qualify under the investor track, you'll generally need to meet these thresholds:

  • Minimum assets: ¥100 million (approximately $670,000 USD) in investable assets
  • Annual income: ¥20 million+ (around $135,000 USD) from investments or business activities
  • Investment commitment: Demonstrable investment in Japanese assets, which can include real estate, securities, or business ventures
  • Clean background: No criminal record and standard immigration health requirements

Important note: Simply buying property alone does not automatically qualify you. The program evaluates your overall financial profile, investment activity, and potential contribution to Japan's economy through a points-based system.

Does Buying Property in Japan Help You Get Residency?

Here's where many investors get confused. Property ownership itself is not a visa category in Japan — anyone, regardless of nationality, can purchase real estate without any residency requirement. A Canadian living in Toronto can buy a Tokyo apartment tomorrow without stepping foot in Japan.

However, real estate investment can strengthen your Golden Visa application in several ways:

1. Demonstrates Financial Commitment

Owning substantial Japanese property (typically ¥50 million or more) shows immigration authorities you have genuine ties to Japan and aren't simply seeking a visa of convenience.

2. Supports Business Manager Visa Applications

If you structure your investment as a property management company, you may qualify for a Business Manager Visa (経営・管理ビザ). This requires:

  • Establishing a Japanese corporation (株式会社 or 合同会社)
  • Initial capital of at least ¥5 million ($33,500 USD)
  • A physical office address in Japan
  • A viable business plan showing profitability

Many foreign investors use this route, purchasing multiple rental properties and managing them through their own company.

3. Generates Qualifying Income

Rental income from Japanese properties counts toward the income requirements for various visa categories, including the Golden Visa's ¥20 million threshold.

Practical Steps to Combine Property Investment with Residency

If you're serious about both investing in Japanese real estate and establishing residency, here's a realistic roadmap:

Step 1: Purchase Property (No Visa Required)

Start by acquiring property that matches your investment goals. Popular options include:

  • Central Tokyo apartments: ¥30-80 million ($200,000-535,000 USD) for rentable 1-2 bedroom units in areas like Shibuya, Shinjuku, or Minato
  • Osaka investment properties: ¥15-40 million ($100,000-270,000 USD) with higher rental yields than Tokyo
  • Vacation rentals in Niseko or Hakuba: ¥50-150 million+ ($335,000-1,000,000 USD) for ski-in/ski-out properties with strong seasonal income

When purchasing, you'll encounter Japan-specific costs including:

  • Shikikin (敷金): Security deposit, typically 1-2 months' rent equivalent
  • Reikin (礼金): "Key money" or gift to the landlord — though this applies more to rentals, commercial purchases have similar customary fees
  • Real estate acquisition tax: 3-4% of the assessed property value
  • Registration and stamp duties: Approximately 1-2% combined

Working with a bilingual real estate agent who understands both Japanese market practices and foreign investor needs can save you significant time and prevent costly misunderstandings during this process.

Step 2: Establish a Japanese Corporation (If Pursuing Business Manager Visa)

This typically costs ¥200,000-500,000 ($1,300-3,350 USD) in registration fees and requires:

  • A registered office address (your property can qualify)
  • Articles of incorporation drafted in Japanese
  • A corporate bank account (increasingly accessible to foreign-owned companies)
  • Appointment of at least one director (can be yourself)

Step 3: Apply for Your Visa

With property owned and/or a business established, work with an immigration lawyer (行政書士 or 弁護士) to submit your application. Processing typically takes 1-3 months.

What the Golden Visa Gets You

Successful applicants receive:

  • 5-year residency status (renewable)
  • Path to permanent residency: Reduced from the standard 10 years to just 1-3 years for qualifying high-skilled professionals
  • Work authorization: Freedom to engage in any business activity
  • Family inclusion: Spouse and dependent children can obtain dependent visas

Tax Considerations for Foreign Property Owners

Japan taxes rental income regardless of your residency status:

  • Non-residents: 20.42% withholding tax on gross rental income
  • Residents: Progressive income tax (5-45%) on net rental income, but with deductions for expenses, depreciation, and mortgage interest

Becoming a tax resident through the Golden Visa can actually reduce your overall tax burden if your properties generate significant income — another factor to discuss with a cross-border tax advisor.

Is Japan's Golden Visa Right for You?

Japan's program suits investors who:

  • Want genuine connection to Japan, not just a "passport in the drawer"
  • Appreciate stable, long-term property appreciation over speculative gains
  • Value quality of life factors like safety, healthcare, and infrastructure
  • Have the financial resources to meet the ¥100 million asset threshold

It's less ideal if you're seeking the fastest or cheapest path to a second residency — countries like Portugal (until recently) or Malaysia offer lower barriers.

Ready to Find Your Perfect Property in Japan?

Navigating Japan's real estate market as a foreigner can be complex — but it doesn't have to be. Our team of bilingual agents specializes in helping expats and international investors find the right property, handle paperwork, and skip the common pitfalls.

Browse Our Listings or Book a Free Consultation — we'll guide you through every step.

excerpt.rendered: Japan's 2025 Golden Visa program offers high-net-worth investors a path to residency, and real estate can play a key supporting role. Learn the requirements, costs, and practical steps to combine property ownership with long-term residency goals.

title.rendered: Remote Work and Living in Japan: Finding the Right Apartment

content.rendered:
Remote Work and Living in Japan: Finding the Right Apartment

The rise of remote work has opened up possibilities that seemed impossible just a few years ago. For many English-speaking professionals, that means finally pursuing the dream of living in Japan — not as a tourist, but as a resident working from a Tokyo apartment or a quiet town in the Japanese countryside.

But finding the right apartment for remote work in Japan comes with unique challenges. Japanese apartments are designed differently, lease requirements can be confusing, and what works for a daily commuter doesn't necessarily work for someone who needs a functional home office.

This guide breaks down everything you need to know about finding a remote-work-friendly apartment in Japan, from understanding Japanese floor plans to budgeting for the real costs involved.

Understanding Japanese Apartment Layouts for Remote Work

Japanese apartments are measured in tatami mats (畳/jō) or square meters, and layouts are described using a specific notation system. A 1LDK means one bedroom plus a combined living, dining, and kitchen area. A 2DK means two rooms plus a dining-kitchen space.

For remote workers, the key question is: where will you actually work?

Minimum Space Requirements

  • 1K or 1R (one-room): Typically 13–25 sqm. Possible for remote work, but your desk will be in your bedroom. Budget option for solo workers.
  • 1LDK: Usually 30–45 sqm. Enough space to set up a desk in the living area, separating work from sleep. This is the sweet spot for most remote workers.
  • 2LDK or larger: 45–70+ sqm. Allows for a dedicated home office room. Ideal if you have video calls at odd hours or need complete separation.

A common mistake is underestimating how small Japanese apartments feel in practice. A 30 sqm 1LDK in Tokyo will feel more cramped than a similarly sized studio in Sydney or New York due to layout differences and lower ceiling heights in some older buildings.

Key Features Remote Workers Should Look For

Not all apartments are created equal when it comes to working from home. Here's what to prioritize:

Internet Infrastructure

Japan generally has excellent internet, but older buildings may only support VDSL connections (50–100 Mbps) rather than fiber-to-the-home. When viewing apartments, ask specifically about hikari kaisen (光回線) — fiber optic availability. Many newer mansion (マンション) buildings (Japanese term for concrete apartment complexes) come with building-wide fiber already installed.

Expect to pay ¥4,000–6,000/month ($27–40 USD) for a separate internet contract, though some buildings include it in rent.

Desk Space and Layout

Look for apartments with:

  • A service room (S) or 納戸 (nando) — a small storage room that can often fit a desk
  • An alcove or dedicated space near windows for natural light
  • Enough electrical outlets (older Japanese apartments often have only 1-2 per room)

Soundproofing Considerations

Japanese walls can be thin, especially in older mokuzō (木造) wooden buildings. For video calls and focused work, prioritize:

  • RC (reinforced concrete) or SRC (steel-reinforced concrete) construction
  • Higher floors (less foot traffic noise)
  • Units that don't share walls with neighbors on both sides

Visa Requirements and Lease Eligibility

Before apartment hunting, you need to understand the visa situation. Japan doesn't currently offer a dedicated "digital nomad visa," but several options exist:

  • Working holiday visa: Available for citizens of Australia, Canada, UK, and other countries (ages 18–30, sometimes 35). Valid for one year.
  • Business manager visa: For those establishing a business in Japan. Requires capital of ¥5 million+.
  • Highly Skilled Professional visa: Point-based system for qualified professionals.
  • Spouse or dependent visa: If married to a Japanese citizen or resident.

Landlords will ask for your zairyū card (在留カード) — your residence card — during the application process. Without a valid visa, you cannot sign a standard lease. Some share houses and short-term rentals work around this, but options are limited.

Real Costs: Budgeting for Your Remote Work Apartment

Japanese rental costs involve more than monthly rent. Here's a realistic breakdown for a 1LDK in central Tokyo (23 wards):

Cost Item Typical Range Notes
Monthly rent ¥100,000–180,000 ($670–1,200) Varies hugely by area
Shikikin 敷金 (security deposit) 1–2 months rent Refundable, minus cleaning
Reikin 礼金 (key money) 0–2 months rent Non-refundable "gift" to landlord
Agency fee 0.5–1 month rent Paid to real estate agent
Guarantor company fee 0.5–1 month rent Required if no Japanese guarantor
Fire insurance ¥15,000–20,000/year Mandatory
Lock change fee ¥15,000–25,000 Often required

Total upfront cost: For a ¥120,000/month apartment, expect to pay ¥400,000–600,000 ($2,700–4,000 USD) before moving in.

More Affordable Options

Outside central Tokyo, costs drop significantly:

  • Saitama, Chiba, Kanagawa suburbs: ¥60,000–100,000/month for 1LDK
  • Regional cities (Fukuoka, Osaka outskirts, Sendai): ¥50,000–80,000/month
  • Rural areas: ¥30,000–50,000/month (but verify internet availability)

Best Areas in Japan for Remote Workers

Tokyo

  • Nakano/Koenji: Creative vibe, affordable, good cafes
  • Monzen-Nakacho: Quieter east side, good value, easy airport access
  • Musashi-Kosugi: Modern, family-friendly, excellent internet infrastructure

Outside Tokyo

  • Fukuoka: Tech hub with startup community, significantly cheaper than Tokyo
  • Kamakura: Coastal town, 1 hour from Tokyo, popular with remote workers
  • Karuizawa: Mountain town, established remote work community, cooler summers

Practical Steps to Find Your Apartment

  1. Determine your visa status and ensure you'll have a residence card before signing a lease
  2. Set your budget including all upfront costs, not just monthly rent
  3. Use English-friendly real estate agencies that specialize in helping foreigners
  4. Visit in person if possible — online photos don't capture noise levels or neighborhood feel
  5. Check internet speeds with the current tenant or building management
  6. Read the contract carefully — some leases prohibit working from home commercially

Final Thoughts

Finding the right apartment for remote work in Japan requires balancing space, location, internet reliability, and budget. The good news is that Japan's rental market has become increasingly foreigner-friendly, and the infrastructure for remote work is excellent once you're set up.

Start your search early, budget for higher upfront costs than you might expect, and prioritize the features that matter most for your work style. With the right apartment, Japan becomes not just a place to visit — but a place to build a life.

EXCERPT: Finding a remote-work-friendly apartment in Japan requires understanding unique lease structures, space requirements, and internet infrastructure. This guide covers everything from Japanese floor plan notation to realistic budgeting for expats planning to work from home in Tokyo or beyond.

META_DESCRIPTION: Guide to finding remote work apartments in Japan: layouts, costs, internet, visas, and best areas for expats working from home in Tokyo and beyond.

KEYWORDS: remote work Japan apartment, work from home Japan, Japan apartment for foreigners, digital nomad Japan, rent apartment Tokyo remote work, Japan expat housing, living in Japan working remotely
